Key events and details:
Initial Problem: Metal chips were found in the engine, requiring a replacement. A borrowed Rolls Royce engine, older in design, was installed incorrectly due to time pressure and a missed service bulletin (2:50-5:55). This led to a fuel tube and hydraulic tube rubbing against each other.
Fuel Leak Detection: The leak started hours into the flight (6:36), but there was no direct sensor to detect it (6:50). The crew noticed unusual oil readings (7:15) and a fuel imbalance (7:39), but misdiagnosed the problem as a computer fault and compounded it by opening a cross-feed valve (8:08).
Engine Failure: The right engine shut down due to fuel starvation (12:20), followed by the left engine 13 minutes later (13:14). This left the plane gliding 144 km from the nearest airport (13:27).
Emergency Landing Preparation: With both engines out, the plane relied on a Ram Air Turbine (RAT) for basic power (14:24). The crew prepared passengers for a possible emergency water landing, but many faced language barriers and issues with life jackets (16:10).
Miraculous Landing: Despite the challenges, Captain Robert Piche, who had previous experience landing planes without runways, successfully glided the plane to the Lajes Airbase (19:51). The landing was rough, causing sparks and minor injuries, but all 306 people onboard survived (20:18).
Aftermath: The incident led to the largest fine in Canadian aviation history against Air Transat and a class-action lawsuit from passengers (21:52). The captain's past as a drug smuggler also came to light (21:21), raising further questions. The video concludes by emphasizing that the disaster was a result of multiple failures, including missing warnings, shortcuts, and ignored safety recommendations (22:33).
